---
title: "5. From Curiosity to a Research Problem"
---

[![Open the lab in Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/assets/colab-badge.svg)](https://colab.research.google.com/github/davi-moreira/2026F_evidence_driven_research_purdue_HONR464/blob/main/notebooks/student/nb01_curiosity_to_problem_student.ipynb){target="_blank"}

> **The research decision.** Which single research problem, out of everything your
> curiosity could become, you will commit a term to. You state it as one
> answerable question that names its units, its outcome, and the population it
> speaks for, and you defend it as important, feasible, and genuinely new.

## Why this decision matters

Picture a research mentor reading your one-line pitch before your first meeting.
She does not ask about your data or your software. She asks one thing: *"What,
exactly, is the question?"* Her reason: *"I can tell in a paragraph whether someone
knows what they are asking. If they do not, nothing downstream saves the project."*

Everything you do later sits below this decision. A vague question makes every
later choice arbitrary, because any data and any answer will seem to fit. A sharp
question tells you what evidence you need and what would count as being wrong. Pick
the problem well and the term has a spine. Pick it badly and no analysis will
straighten it.

## The concept

Turning curiosity into a problem is a funnel with four stops. Each stop throws
away vagueness the stop above it still allowed.

- **Curiosity**: a felt interest with no edges yet. Example: *"why do some people
  seem to get sick more during exam weeks?"* You cannot collect evidence on it,
  because it never says who or what to look at.
- **Topic**: a curiosity with edges, a territory someone could gather evidence
  inside. Example: *"sleep and illness among residence-hall first-years."*
- **Research problem**: one specific unknown inside that territory whose absence
  matters. Example: *"we do not know whether short sleep during finals week goes
  with more reported illness."*
- **Answerable question**: the single sentence evidence can answer, and be wrong
  about. Example: *"do first-years who sleep under six hours during finals report
  more sick days than those who sleep seven or more?"*

A good question also carries three things it must name, or you will not know what
to record.

- **Units of analysis**: the who or what each row of your evidence stands for.
  Example: one student. If you cannot say what one unit is, you cannot count
  anything.
- **Target population**: the full group the question is really about, beyond the
  handful you reach. Example: all residence-hall first-years, not only the twenty
  you surveyed.
- **Outcome**: the observable thing that varies, the quantity you actually record.
  Example: the number of self-reported sick days. It must be measurable, even if
  imperfectly.

Finally, three tests decide whether a candidate problem is worth a term.
**Importance** asks whether anyone beyond you would care about the answer.
**Feasibility** asks whether you can get the evidence this term, with the access
and time you have. **Contribution** asks whether the answer adds something not
already settled, since a ten-minute search that finds it already known kills the
contribution.

Once you have a question, you will also classify what *kind* of answer it wants.
That is the inquiry compass, and it is the whole job of the next chapter. For now,
the work is the funnel.

## A worked example

Start with a health curiosity: *"does moving more actually help you sleep?"* It is
a real feeling, but you cannot study it, because it names no one and measures
nothing. Give it edges and it becomes a **topic**: physical activity and sleep
quality in undergraduates. Push further to a **research problem**: we do not know
whether more daily walking goes with better-rated sleep among first-years living
in residence halls. That points at one missing piece, not a whole subject.

Now sharpen it into an **answerable question**: *"among residence-hall first-years,
do those who average more than 8,000 steps a day report better sleep quality than
those who average fewer than 4,000?"* The three carried terms snap into place. The
**unit** is one first-year. The **outcome** is a sleep-quality rating, say a
1-to-10 self-report you collect on a phone. The **population** is residence-hall
first-years, not the whole campus.

Now run the three tests. It clears **importance** (anyone advising sleep-deprived
first-years wants the answer), **feasibility** (step counts and a nightly rating
are both collectable in one term), and **contribution** (the activity-and-sleep
link is studied broadly in adults but far less in this dorm-living, first-year
group). The curiosity is now a problem you could defend.

## The Colab laboratory

This chapter's lab is notebook **nb01 — From curiosity to a research problem:
descriptive, predictive, causal**. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work
the cells alongside this chapter. You will watch one curiosity sharpen the instant
it touches real survey columns, then walk your own curiosity from your nb00 map
down the funnel, draft two candidate problems, and defend the one you keep.

## Recommended AI prompts

Commit your own answer first. Only then open the tool, so its output has something
of yours to disagree with.

**Locate what is already known** (before you claim your problem is new):

```
Act as a research-scoping assistant. My topic is [your topic]. List what is
already reasonably well established about it and where a genuine open question
might remain. For every established fact, give one real, retrievable source I
can open myself. Mark anything you are unsure exists.
```

*After running, verify:* open each source before you trust it. This counters
**confident fabrication**, where a fluent answer cites a real-sounding study that
does not exist.

**Widen a curiosity into candidates you check:**

```
Here is my curiosity: [one sentence]. Produce five candidate research problems
inside it. For each, name its units of analysis, its outcome, and its target
population, in a table. Do not add any topic I did not mention.
```

*After running, verify:* check each row against the curiosity you actually wrote.
This counters **silent scope change**, where the tool quietly answers a nearby
question and hands it back as yours.

**Red-team the problem you chose:**

```
Act as a hostile grant reviewer. Here is the research problem I want to commit a
term to: [paste it]. Argue the strongest case that it is not worth doing. Attack
it on importance, on feasibility, and on whether it adds anything new.
```

*After running, verify:* keep only the objections that survive your own reasoning.
This counters **sycophantic agreement**, where a tool praises your idea because you
proposed it.

::: {.callout-important title="Do not delegate"}
Three calls stay yours alone. **Which problem** you commit a term to. **How you
word** the question, including its units, outcome, and population. And the
**judgment** on whether it is important and genuinely new. A tool can widen your
options and attack your choice. It cannot make the choice, because only you carry
the reasons you will have to defend at the studio.
:::

## An AI failure case

You ask a tool to *"turn my curiosity about sleep and health into a research
question."* It replies, with total confidence: *"Does blue-light exposure before
bed reduce REM sleep in college undergraduates? Studies show blue light suppresses
melatonin."* It sounds authoritative, and it is wrong for you.

It fails in two ways. First, it silently changed your scope. You never chose blue
light or REM sleep, yet the question now hangs on both. That is a
**silent scope change**, and you catch it by reading the tool's units, outcome, and
population against the ones you committed. Yours were steps and a sleep rating, not
screens and REM. Second, the melatonin line is stated as settled fact with no
source. Before repeating it, you find and open a real study yourself, and if you
cannot, you treat it as **confident fabrication** and drop it.

## Verification laboratory

The key result of this chapter is a claim about your own question: that it is
answerable and carries its three terms. Verify it with **peer reasoning**, method
11 in the [verification guide](../../ai_resources/verification_guide.md). Hand your
one-sentence question to a classmate who has never seen your project, and ask them
to name, from that sentence alone, what one unit is, what you would record, and who
the answer is about. If they name all three, your question holds. If they stall,
the question is not yet answerable, and their confusion shows which term is still
missing.

## Project transfer

This chapter builds the spine of milestone **M1, your research opportunity
landscape**. Take the strongest curiosity from your nb00 map and walk it down the
funnel: topic, research problem, then answerable question, naming its units,
outcome, and population, and scoring it on importance, feasibility, and
contribution. What you defend at the Friday studio is a problem you funneled and
chose yourself, not a tool's suggestion.

## Defend your decision

In three or four sentences, state the one problem you are committing to as an
answerable question, and defend it. Name the **boundary**: one thing this question
will *not* let you claim, even if you answer it well. Name your **uncertainty**:
the part of importance, feasibility, or contribution you are least sure of, and
what evidence would settle it. A choice you can defend with its edge and its doubt
named is a research problem. A choice without them is still a curiosity.
